title: Far Breton
subtitle: Breton prune flan baked with a custardy batter
excerpt: Far Breton is a baked Breton flan: a thick, custardy batter poured over pitted prunes in a buttered dish and baked until deeply browned and set. It is somewhere between a clafoutis and a thick pancake. The batter is simple and quick to mix; the oven does the rest. It is served at room temperature, sliced into squares.
sourceNotes: Far Breton is a traditional Breton dessert, documented in Brittany since at least the nineteenth century. The name 'far' derives from the Latin 'far' meaning grain or flour, reflecting its origins as a wheat-based porridge. The prune version is the most widely made. It is eaten at room temperature, often served at Breton fairs and markets. This recipe is synthesised from the established Breton baking tradition.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Far Breton keeps for two days covered at room temperature and actually improves the day after baking.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan or conventional; the top should turn a deep, even amber before the batter is fully set

glossaryTerms.definition: Far Breton batter is denser than a crepe or clafoutis batter but flows freely when poured. It is made by whisking flour and sugar with eggs first to prevent lumps, then adding the warm milk gradually. Resting for 30 minutes allows the flour to hydrate fully and gives a smoother, more even bake. The finished far should be about 3 to 4 cm deep, set all the way through but still fudgy in the centre, and very dark on top. If the top is pale, cook it for longer.

content.text: rests for 30 minutes before baking. This is not optional: the flour hydrates during the rest and the batter thickens slightly, giving a more even, fudgy texture in the finished far. Do not skip this step even if the batter looks smooth enough.

items.prepNote: melted, plus extra for the tin

content.text: Soak the prunes and make the batter

If using brandy, pour it over the prunes and leave to soak while you make the batter. Whisk together the 200 g flour and 150 g sugar in a bowl. Add the 4 eggs one at a time, whisking until smooth after each. Add the 30 g melted butter and vanilla. Pour in the 600 ml warm milk gradually, whisking until completely smooth. Leave to rest for 30 minutes.

Heat the oven to 190 degrees C. Butter a 23 cm round cake tin or a gratin dish generously. Scatter the prunes over the base. Pour the rested batter over the prunes. Bake for 40 to 45 minutes until the top is very deep amber, the edges are set, and the centre has only a slight wobble. Cool in the tin for 20 minutes before turning out. Serve at room temperature, cut into slices.

items.solution: The batter was over-mixed after adding the flour, developing too much gluten. Mix until just smooth, then rest without further stirring.

items.problem: Top is pale and batter is not fully set
items.solution: The oven was not hot enough, or the far was taken out too soon. The top must be a deep, even amber before you pull it. Add 5 to 10 minutes and check again.
